title: Flow-Based Global Proposals for Monte Carlo Sampling in SU(2) Lattice Gauge Theory
summary: We propose a formally valid machine-learning-assisted global proposal mechanism for Monte Carlo sampling in lattice gauge theory. The construction is based on a coupling-flow update on the SU(2) lattice-link manifold, in which active links are transformed conditionally on a frozen-link background. For fixed frozen links, the proposal is explicitly invertible and preserves the product Haar measure, so it can be embedded into a Metropolis-Hastings correction without requiring an explicit model of the full proposal density. We implement the method in two-dimensional pure SU(2) lattice gauge theory and benchmark it against a baseline local Metropolis algorithm used as a controlled reference kernel. In the present testbed, the learned proposal reproduces the target ensemble within statistical resolution across the tested configurations. In matched local-step comparisons, the learned proposal reproduces the target ensemble at a quality comparable to the baseline, but does not outperform the pure local baseline in the conservative matched-step case examined with seed-level statistics within this proof-of-principle setup. At the same time, a favorable mixed-step hybrid configuration yields a modest improvement in effective sample size per unit runtime. Because the learned transformation remains in a near-identity regime, the present results should be interpreted as a proof-of-principle demonstration of formal correctness and limited, configuration-dependent efficiency gain within a controlled comparison, rather than as evidence of superiority over optimized conventional update schemes. This work provides a concrete foundation for extending machine-learned nonlocal updates to larger lattices and non-Abelian gauge theories relevant to lattice QCD.

AI.tldr: A formally valid machine-learning-assisted global proposal mechanism for Monte Carlo sampling in lattice gauge theory is proposed, based on a coupling-flow update. It's implemented and tested in 2D SU(2) lattice gauge theory, showing formal correctness and limited efficiency gains, setting the stage for future work in larger lattices and non-Abelian gauge theories.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a machine learning-based method that can improve or at least offer an alternative to traditional Monte Carlo sampling techniques used in lattice gauge theory, particularly for complex systems like QCD where nonlocal updates could be beneficial.
AI.method: The method involves a coupling-flow update on the SU(2) lattice-link manifold, with active links being transformed conditionally on a frozen-link background. The transformation is designed to preserve the product Haar measure and be explicitly invertible, allowing it to be embedded into a Metropolis-Hastings algorithm without needing the full proposal density. The approach was tested against a local Metropolis algorithm in 2D pure SU(2) lattice gauge theory.
AI.result: The learned proposal mechanism successfully reproduces the target ensemble within statistical resolution, and while it does not outperform the baseline in a matched-step comparison, a mixed-step hybrid configuration provides a modest improvement in effective sample size per unit runtime. The study remains in a near-identity regime, which limits the observed efficiency gains.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the machine learning-assisted proposal mechanism is formally correct and shows potential for application in more complex scenarios, such as larger lattices and non-Abelian gauge theories, although it does not yet demonstrate clear superiority over conventional methods. This work sets a foundation for further exploration and development of nonlocal updates in lattice field theories.
